question: You are analyzing the rise of international co-productions in cinema. Identify films that involved production companies from multiple countries, focusing on the period 1995-2015. Characterize how multi-country productions differ from single-country productions in terms of genre mix, typical runtime, cast diversity (measured by nationality of cast members via `aka_name`), and critical reception. Reach a defensible conclusion about whether multi-country co-productions systematically outperform or underperform single-country productions on quality metrics available in the database. When you have enough evidence, state your final conclusion and stop.
final_answer:
The genre+vote tier breakdown is the decisive analysis. The deltas are mixed — multi-country wins in some cells (Drama low-vote, Documentary, Horror), single-country wins in others (Action 100-999, Crime 1K+, Animation 100-999, Comedy 1K+). There is no consistent directional advantage.

DONE

---

## Summary: Multi-Country Co-Productions (1995–2015)

### Scale
- 161,443 single-country films (82%), 35,372 multi-country (18%) among feature-length and short films with company data in the period.

### Genre Mix
Multi-country productions skew toward Drama (23%), Comedy (13%), Thriller (8%), and Documentary (7%). Single-country productions have a massive Short film component (32% vs 6%), which must be excluded for fair comparison.

### Runtime
After excluding shorts, multi-country features are ~7.7 minutes longer on average (99.3 vs 91.6 min), consistent with bigger-budget, more ambitious productions.

### Cast Diversity
Multi-country films have nearly twice the cast size (28.6 vs 15.6 average cast members) and a higher share of internationally-named actors (40.8% vs 32.2% with `aka_name` entries), confirming genuine cross-border casting.

### Critical Reception — The Core Finding
| Level of analysis | Multi-country | Single-country | Delta |
|---|---|---|---|
| Raw average (all films incl. shorts) | 5.755 | 5.489 | +0.27 |
| Non-shorts only | 5.866 | 5.326 | +0.54 |
| Within same vote bucket (100–999 votes) | 5.572 | 5.568 | +0.004 |
| Within same vote bucket (1K–9.9K votes) | 6.042 | 6.180 | −0.14 |

The apparent rating advantage **collapses when controlling for vote count**. Multi-country productions attract far more IMDB votes (higher global visibility), and higher-voted films naturally have more stable/higher ratings due to sample size effects. Within matched vote tiers and genres, the delta oscillates around zero with no consistent direction — Action, Crime, Animation, and Comedy all show single-country films rating *higher* at the 1K+ vote tier, while Drama and Documentary show slight multi-country advantages at low vote counts.

### Conclusion
**Multi-country co-productions do not systematically outperform single-country productions on quality metrics.** The raw rating gap is an artifact of popularity/visibility bias: co-productions attract larger global audiences (more votes), which inflates their apparent ratings. Once popularity is controlled for, and comparing within the same genre and audience-size bracket, the quality signal is indistinguishable. What co-productions *do* demonstrably differ on is scale — longer runtimes, larger and more internationally diverse casts — reflecting greater resources and cross-border ambition, not higher intrinsic quality.
